CURRENT AFFAIRS.

" To bank clerks and peelers we must now add a station-
master. A Mr. Pepper held the position of station-
master at Nenagh inGallant Tip., on the Great Sourface
. Railway, for many years. Mr. Pepper, as his name may

~ be said to imply, is one of the ‘‘Saved,”” and we have no

g doubt he was one of the white-haired coons of the
Great Sourface Company. He is about to retire into
private life, if he has not already done so.  The refire-
ment of such a great public man of Nenagh could not be
allowed to take place without the town being moved in
its heart and in its pockets. And so one Dr. H. F.
Powell, J.P., occupied the chair in a room in & hqensed

tablishment, when an informal meeting considered
the weighty matter of taking steps to present a suitable
testimonial to the Pepper man, and thereby show the

esteem in which the true and independent people of
Nenagh and district held this Great Sourface under-
strapper. A Mr. H. J. Sheehan is hon. sec. pro tem.
By the way, who will the Great Sourface Railway put
into the vacancy caused by the retirement of Pepper?
Some ‘‘Saved "’ Ginger from the North maybe ? When
Nenagh has testimonialed a ‘‘Saved’’ Pepper, what can
the Nenagh half-slaves say if the Great Sourface send
down some ‘‘Saved '’ Ginger from the North to fill the
vacancy ?

What about protection for the scaltered Papists and
Nationalists of the South and West? Our readers at
least know how badly the Papists need protection, but
the cry of protection for the scattered *“ Saved’’ is kept
up with such persistency that even tame Nationalists
must in weak moments suspect that they are dreadful
persecutors. In the town of Mountmellick the chair-
man and members of the Town Commissioners orga-
nised a procession and public meeting in honour of the
passing of the second reading of the Home Rule Bill.
When some people were returning from the proceedings
dirty fluid matter was thrown upon them from one of
the windows of a Protestant business house in the town.
It is to the credit of the Mountmellick Town Commis-
sioners that when the news of the dirty attack became
public a special meeting was called to consider the
matter. The dirty attack was delivered from the pre-
mises of Messrs. Humphrey, Smyth and Sons, general
merchants.  If dirty fluid were thrown out of a
Nationalist house in Portadown on an Orange procession
what would happen? It was decided to write to the
firm asking for an explanation.

THE KING TO THE RESCUE.

(An Irish Unionist’s graceful conception of how His
Majesty should behave when the Home Rule Bill
is brought to him for signature. Apology is made
for not making the picture half vulgar enough.)

Scene—A Room in Buckingham Palace.

(Enter the King, Carson, Craig, Castlereagh, Moore
and others.) 5

Carson (aside)—His royal Majesty looks very grim. -
Craig (do.)—He hath assumed an aspect very grum.
And looks most like a Sourface. !
Castle. (do.)—The sword of danger that is hanging o'er
Poor loyal Irish Protestants hath made
His Gracious most pugnacious.
Moore (do.)—The King will fight,
And His Majesty will be right.
King—The Carrion Crow is hoarse .
That croaks the fatal danger of Home Rule
To Ireland’s loyal Protestants. Come, come, - i
you spirits, -
That animated William at the Boyne,
And rouse me to an opposition dire
Against this menace of democracy.
Come steel my heart in enmity against
The foul enactments of base knaves conjoined
In rebel coalition. Ha, loyal friends,
We will not have Home Rule. !

All—Never, your Majesty. < 5
King—Brothers, will we sever!

All—Never, never, never,
But go on forever for Union and for King.

~-established a footing in Dingle.

trample under foot: 2
_ The foolish doctrine that doth set the King
Above all party politics. I here unmask
And show myself a Unionist most grim.
. God save the Union.
All—And our gracious King.
(Enter a lord.)

~ Lord—Most mighty sovereign,

The First Lord of the Treasury has come—
King—Off with his head—
I mean allow the traitor to approach
Our royal presence.
(Enter Asquith.)
Asquith—My gracious liege,
I bear a golden message to thy hand
From Ireland’s injured people.
King—Tell that to the marines.
The Irish people stand around me here—
At least those Irish that I care about—
In Craig and Carson, Castleveagh and Moore,
And they have not commissioned you to bear
A message from them.
Carson—Your Majesty may bet your eye on that.
Asquith—T speal of millions, not of venal few,
‘Who malke a cash-box of your royal throne.
I do beseech your Majesty to sign
The Home Rule Bill.
King—Go, light your pipe with.it.
Asquith—Am I to understand
Your royal Grace doth flout the people’s will?
King—I flout no people’s will,
Because the people who do follow you
Possess no will that they can call their own.
The only men who have a mind at all
Are English Tories and the Trish ** Saved *’;
So take your Bill to Jericho.
Asquith—Your Majesty, beware
King—Seize the traitor,
He threatens me.

(Carson and the others collar the Prime Minister.)
Carson—Oh, here’s a job at which I’d never tire.
Craig—Shall we the traitor put in irons, sire!
King—Chain him up and take him to the Tower

S Within an iron cage. Then come again
And meet me here before an hour has passed
When we of warlike matters will confer.
Fly flags, flash signals, warning tocsins ring.

We must be brief when traitors brave the King.”

Flourish curtain.

- We have had some notes on the Dingle peninsula in
recent issues. Dingle is feeling some of the forces of
Anglicisation, and Irish Ireland has its work cut out
for it in“the peninsula. Has Dingle a Vigilance Com-
mittee, and if not, why not? Though we have not
first hand knowledge on the matter, we fear it is too
much .to expect that the * penny dirties ’ have not
One reason we have
for holding this opinion is a collection of imported post-

- cards that a correspondent bought in a shop in Dingle,

and that are now before us. The-young people of

Dingle, we understand, are chiefly the purchasers of

these postecards. What are the police doing? But,

%)egal geﬁnitions {olg’avolve police interference. And in
any case the country would be m'bad case that had to
depend on the police to defend its morals. Why is
there not a Vigilance Committee in Dingle ? Places like
Dingle, that are in the first stages of ta_pld Anghcls.u,tlor_x,
are very much predisposed toall that is bad and dirty in
the dirtier sides of English modern life. The English-
man is so used to dirt that, to some extent, ‘he almo_sb
becomes immune; to the Dingle gentry of a certain
type the double-meaning posteard and the dirty sug-
gestions have the virulence of novelty infecting an
already weakened character. But quite a little centre
of moral dirt is a dangerous thing in a place like Dxpgle,
and it is shocking to contemplate the open sale in Dingle
of those dirty postcards with their obvious double-
meaning inscriptions. i

It is hard to keep patience with those who take so
casily this spread of dirt in Ireland. ~ Whilst under
their eyes, if they would only open them, they must
seo the dirty seeds that are being sown broadcast, and
that in time, as sure as effect follows cause, must spring
up, have already sprung up, into very ugly fruit. A
town, even in such a sequestered corner of Ireland as
Dingle, in which dirty posteards are retailed, may be
expected to be witness to other classes of dirt if investi-
gation is carried far enough. It is certainly time that a
Vigilance Committee were started in the place, for
these postcards are symptoms—if there were no further
evidence—that this chief town in one of the remaining

" strongholds of the Gael needed men on the look out for

something worse than icebergs.  The, first session of
the Irish College in Dingle opens.on July 8th, and we
hope, indeed we may rest assured once their attention
is called to it, that the students will give positive proof
that Irish Ireland stands for cleanliness by attempting
to rid Dingle of dirty postcards and other dirt, if the
Dingle stable is not cleansed by the time the session
opens. 7

- We recently received from a gentleman who called to
our office a complaint about University College, Cork,
in connection with its playing into the hands of Lenden
University. We did not get enough parliculars, and
the gentleman has not since called or communicated
with us. We hope, however, shortly to get some
printed documentary evidence to go upon. But we
know enough to satisfy ourselves that there is a playing
into the hands of London University on the part of the
University College, presided over by Wobbling Windle.
It is & long time now since we referred to Windle; in-
deed, we do not think we have noticed him since he has
been exalted to the social level of his illustrious fellow-
citizen, Sir Daniel Hegarty. By next week we may
have sufficient evidence to enable us to deal with the
relations between University College, Cork, and London
University ; bub as the current issue of the ‘“ University
College, Cork, Official Gazette *’ is before us, we can
deal with the College’s attempt to recruit students for
Wales. In the issue before us there is an article on
‘ Agricultural Instruction.”” We cannot say if Windle
is the author of the article, but it is ‘‘ published under
the authority of the Governing Body.”’ It is an article
that might have been written by a London journalist

who had never seen anything further approaching a real

cow than a ‘‘ chaney ’* cow in the window of a dairy. -

During the first year, in addition

Bub the pracfical side of his instruction will by

receive instruction in horticulture of all kinds, and

. especially in the care and management of fruit

experienced and practical teacher. He will also
have an opportunity of studying the care and
management of bees.

Perhaps, Wobbling Windle will teach students to grow
periwinkles in grass plots. .And what about the
management of wasps?

But the real sting is in the suggestions for the second
vear. Clonakilty is too near to Cork city for the small
mind of Windle to be anything but negligible. ~ The
Albert Farm at Glasnevin is too near the sod for a
new knight. We suppose the Franciscan Agricultural
College at Mount Bellew is also beneath the notice of
the social equal of the illustrious Sir Daniel Hegarty.
Knights are cheap to-day! The *‘ Official Gazette” of
Wobbling Windle’s College suggests that Agricultural
students of Cork should go to North Wales! They don’t
speak with a Cork accent there; redundant consonants
will no doubt in due course be tone in ‘‘ Cawk,”’ and
even Blackpool will be able to give the proper blas
to such names as Llanelly. We quote from the *‘Official
Gazette " :—

The Second Year will be spent away from the
College. Subject to the approval of the University,
the student will be enabled to select his own Agri-
cultural College. It ‘must be one where practical
farm work can be carried out, and where instruc-
tion—theoretical and practical—in Agriculture,

ry, Agricultural Drawing and Building Con-
on, and Practical and Agricultural Chemis-
y,-can be obtained. There are, of course, a
number of such Colleges. ~ As an’ example, that
connected with the University College of North
Wales can be cited. This College has a farm of
675 acres, of which more than a-third is arable and
pasture land of fair quality, well suited for the
growth of a diversity of crops. The remainder is
an upland sheep walk. Owing to its varied char-
acter, the farm offers great facilities to students
for acquiring a knowledge of different kinds of farm-
ing. Bxperiments are conducted on the growth of
various farm-crops, and on the breeding and feed-
ing of different classes of live stock. A special
feature is made of stock-breeding, and horses and
catitle and sheep from the farm are exhibited from
time to time ‘at the principal shows. In such a
College as this, with such a farm attached, all the

can be obtained.

A correspondent writes:—‘* The University College,
Cork, is going to have a sports meeting. It is a branch
of the new ‘ National’ University, and as such we
would expect that it would show itself somewhat more
national than the old Queen’s College.  But no, the
sports are to be ‘claussy,’ and, being ‘ claussy,’” mili-
tary bands are to be employed, though Cork has some
splendid civilian bands.  Speaking to a vrofessor of
the College during the week, we broached this sub-
ject; and expressed our surprise. He {old us, in
answer, that there was not a spark of nationality at-
tached to the College. * Even youths,” said he
warmly, fresh from the country, where they never
saw any game but hurling, are worse than the born
West-Briton, and are the chief opponents to hurling
in the College.” And this is the National University,
which was to put a soul into Ireland. No wonder that
the little St. Colman’s students were abie to defeat the
College Goliaths at Hurling last week. That professor
is a sincere friend of Irish Ireland, and we were not
surprised when he said : ‘These are the people whom the
ratepayers are helping to educate.”  And we could
not help thinking.  Yes, helping to educate, not as
Trishmen and women, but as Waest-Britons, more
English than the English themselves.”

The Dublin Suffers commenced their series of out-
door meetings in the Pheenix Park on Saturday evening.
Barring the Dublin mountains, they could not hﬂ:ve
gone to @ better place within easy reach of Dublin.
So long as they confine themselves to henpecking their
husbands, their sweethearts and the Pheenix Park, the
public will take them smilingly.

demand votes for women.” ‘‘ Well,” said Lloyd

We were at a Hurling match on Saturday, Leinster
Colleges against Munster Colleges. A Turling match
is a fine thing to look at, far and away more interest-
ing than one of these international Rugby Football
contests.  There is more play, more dash, and more
excitement from the spectators’ point of view. Need-
less to say, there is not a line about the match in the
‘“ Irish Times,’’ though the ““ Freeman’’ devotes nearly
a column to it. We wonder did the ¢ Irish Times’
ever menfion such a thing as a Hurling match? We
did not see any of the Professors or other highly-
salaried officials of TUniversity College, Dublin,
there. Watching Cricket or backing gee-gees
at the Pheenix Park would be more in their
line, we suppose. But very Rev. Canon Barrett, of

INDUSTRY, 3

At a meeting of the Birr Town Tenants recently one
of the members made some references to the need for
supporting Irish manufacture. The population of Bin:
has gone down. He stated that there were no in-
dustries in the town. If so, how can it be expected
to do anything but decay? The necessary distributors
are as much an element of economic production as the
man with the spade or the machine minder, and places
like Birr will, no doubt, always have a purpose to serve
as necessary distributing centres.  But the circum-
stances of distribution are changing greatly in recent
years so far as Ireland is concerned. Distribution
tends to economise by perfecting its organization, and
where ten huxters eked out a living twenty years ago,
oné well organised store, employing a number of assis-
tants, tends to cover as big a distributing ground. The
standard of comfort is going up in Ireland, and towns
that lived on huxtering must decay and give support
only to a lesser number of better organised traders
unless fresh outlets for economic energy are made for
the labour displaced.  This is true apart altogether
from any tendency of famers to run their own grocery
stores—a tendency about which there is far more talk

At the meeting there was a reference to Irish cement.
We-are thorougﬁly sick of reading references to Irish
cement. There is one Dark Brother in all Ireland
making cement, and his output is a comparatively
small thing.  The production, or rather non-produc-
tion, of an adequate supply of Irish cement is the
question, and not the support of the little bit that is
produced, and at best can only go a little way. This
matter has been before the public almost as long as the
LEeADER, and yet there has been no effort to come to
close quarters with it.  There was, we think, talk
some years ago of cement manufacture in Limerick,
and we think also in Cork, but nothing materialised.
There is obviously a clear field for the cement industry
in Ireland. Unfortunately demand in this case does
not create supply, for the demand is there any time
this ten years. Is there no man in Ireland of capital,
or man who can command capital, and who is likewise
competent to establish cement making?

And when may we expect some development in the
Irish slate industry? We don’t know whether the
gentleman who once got a gold medal for mathematics
is still a director of the Killaloe Slate Co., but it were
time that a few other slate companies were started in
Ireland.

I N the spring a young man’s fancy waistcoat changes
to a brighter iris. Thoughts of dress become even
more recurrent in the mind of the maiden than duzing &
3 harsher season. Poets assert that it is because nature
- is donning a new garb that humanity desires to follow
suit. [The pun is base, but punning is now the mode.
This is worse; but the greatest intellects of the age
have set the fashion.] The Poets, of course, are wrong.
If their theory were correct, then we would go unclad
in winter, because the trees are bare. But the sun is
shining brighter, the days are longer, and sometimes
there is heat; therefore, we put on our best attire and
oull out a holiday—or a half-holiday, when we can—or
when we must. It is fitting, then, at this season to
reflect on raiment.

about whom there exists no doubt, save a doubt as to
whether he existed, gives a sanction and authority to
the dullest platitude. 3

Now if I had written ‘‘ Don't judge a book by the
cover,” nobody would Lknow that I had read Shake-
speare. It would be erroneously supposed that I de-
pended for my platitudes on the copy-books of my
youth. Yet that simple sentence conveys more truth-
fully the views I would fain put forward than the
Elizabethan quotation. Not that the advice it contains
is literally true. In point of fact a book may much
more accurately be judged by its cover than a man by
his clothes. The apparel oft proclaims the man to be
something which he is not.

In Dublin we follow Tondon fashions. That is what -
we think, anyhow, and the charge is mostly true in the
case of our women. The very last extremes of costume
affected by English leaders of society may not be ac-
curately copied in all their ugliness by our social leaders.
but none the less these do pretty well. Mankind is not
so successful. The sillt hat and the braided morning-
coat are rarely encountered in our streets. Here and
there they dazzle by their splendour. An occasional
lawyer, a fashionable physician, may excite envy now
and again by such displays. For the most part, how-
ever, the lounge-suit and the bowler hat or soothing
“ Homburg ' is the favourite costume of our citizens.

It is of such clothes as these and of their wearers that
I would speak. Similar in their general outlines, yet
infinite is the variety of shapes and hues they can
assume. And it is in dealing with the men clad in
these lounge-suits that one has torefuse to permit one’s
judgment to be misled by the externals.  True, the
correctly-garbed lawyer may not have cases and suits as
well as a suit-case ; true, the elegant doctor may not be
able to point to the brass-plates on his victims’ coffins
as well as to the brass-plate on his hall-door.  There
may be no gold where there is glitter. But as I have
observed these phenomena are few, and so we may
disregard them.

Age can wither and custom stale a lounge suit. This
I know, moi qui parle. But it does not always foliow
in Dublin that the wearer of a withered suitis a man of
slender means. (It does sometimes; this, too, I know).
Men of substance in Dublin, and I.am told all over
Ireland, are often shabby. Men of straw very often
are most particular, chief in this matter of attire. Take

May 25, 1912.

I started, half-a-hundred strong, from the

1onao comne.  The secretary strode in front.
Modern degeneracy had not reached him. The rotun-
dity of his calves had the sanction of -antiquity, and.
his' brogues were nailed for the rough road and the
craggy climb.  His blue eye blazéd ’neath a flutter-
ing"fringe of hair, his unwrinkled brow gleanied white
in the dusty sunlight, and he had his cap in his pocket.
Behind him two fiddlers, young in yeurs but old in
tunes, trudged tortuously, their music sheathed and
their shoulders drooping with the weight of their fiddle-
boxes.  Behind camo we—merry maidens and hale
old men, youths of every age, and married ladies not
a few, struggling along in anything but file and in
everything but step, some laughing uproariously, some
smiling laboriously, all perspiring profusely.  And as
the country opened before us—the breathing wood-
lands, the glinting streams, the spreading pastures
with contented cows chewing an interminable cud as
they lay buried to the horns in .their ernerald carpet,
the thrush fluting on the branch and the robin twit-
tering in the braie, and above all, the lark in the clear
air, where the mountain peaks kept sentinel—we felt,
every one of us, that they, every one of them, were
getting fresher and fresher every mile, while we and
the dusty road were getting more tired and more tired.
Two hours of it brought us to a bridge, and unani-
mously we sat on the battlements exhausted. 3

On the tramp you couldn’t easily catch what lan-
guage the crowd was speaking now, in the quiet, you
recognised the sibilant lispings of the Gall. ‘‘ Have
we got far more to go? ’’ somebody usked nervously.

““Oh, no, ’tis just the next turn of the road,” was
the answer of the riant secretary, and, with a glance
at his watch, out he strode again.

We followed tamely. He hadn’t exactly lied. ‘It
was the next turn of the road, but the road was per-
fectly straight for about a mile at that particular point.
And then he led us up a boreen and across fields and
finally up a mountain. ~When we got near:the top a
house appeared in view. = ‘‘ Here we are,”” said the
secretary.

We were there right enough, but as we sat down and
looked affrighted back over the endless streak of white
road we had tramped, with the church-spire that had
been barely discernible in the distance in front of us
when we started, now barely discernible in the dis-
tance behind us, we wished"we were somewhere else.
Presently, however, the cry: ‘“ Here it is’’ dispelled
dull care. ““It’’ was the tea.

Hunger and thirst unmask the Adam and Eve in- all
of us. Within the next few minutes I saw pallid poets
cating griddle bread by the junk and drinking black
tea out of mugs. I saw artists hawking four-gallon
black kettles up that hill, and pouring tea, that literally
was eating and drinking, out of them all over the half-
acre of sprawling humanity. T saw lump-sugar pro-
duced lump by lump from ladies’ pockets and dis-
solved with the aid of hat-pins for spoons. I saw con-

We wish to draw the attention of our correspondents
to the fact that next Monday being a Bank Holiday we
would like editorial communications to be sent to us
earlier than usual.

We hear that the Postmastership of Cork is vacant.

filling of the vacancy.

_ The Insurance Commissioners for Ireland hope to
issue a first list of Approved Societies during the first
week in June. At the present time no society in Ire-
land is entitled to call itself an Approved Society.

SITUATION VACANT.

The Committee of Management of An Cumann
Upaoair (Irish Ireland Benefit Society) will, at their
meeting on the 31st day of May, 1912, consider appli-
cations for the position of secretary.  Other things
being equal, a decided preference will be given to
applicants having a knowledge of Irish. The remune-
ration for the position will be probably between £100
and £300 per annum, but will depend upon the mem-
bership of the society. The appointment made will
be subject to the ratification of the Board of Directors
shortly to be elected.  Applications, with copies of
testimonials, are to be sent to the City of Dublin Press
Agency, 12 D’Olier Street, Dublin, before 12 noon on
31st May.
